turns.content: I'm reviewing the histogram configuration for our new service, but I thought setting the p99 bucket at 100ms would guarantee zero latency spikes above that threshold. Is that actually how Rust metrics work?

That's a common misconception with histograms; the buckets define ranges, not hard limits. If you set p99 at 100ms, any request slower than that will still fall into a bucket and contribute to the count, just in a higher range bin. The real value comes from querying the histogram for percentiles like p50 or p99 using the data collected so far.
Got it, so I should be bucketing by latency ranges instead of fixed thresholds? And when alerting on high-percentile latency, do I need to use a specific trait or just read from the stored histogram?

Yes, you define buckets as ranges like 0-10ms, 10-50ms, etc. For alerting on high percentiles in Rust, you typically use the metrics crate which wraps prometheus::HistogramVec and implements the std::error::Error trait for reporting. You can then compute p99 by sorting the collected samples or using a percentile library like approx::percentile.
Wait, so if I'm using prometheus in my side project, do I need to manually implement Serialize for my custom histogram type before pushing it to the server?

Not necessarily; the metrics crate handles serialization to Prometheus format automatically when you call register or collect. However, if you're building a custom metric type that isn't part of the standard library, yes, you'll need to implement Serialize via serde_json::Value or use the derive macro from serde_derive to make it serializable.
Okay, so my custom latency tracker should probably inherit from metrics::Histogram but I want to know if there's a way to get raw sample counts without going through the full exposition format?

You can access the internal samples via the HistogramVec's internal storage if you implement Debug or use the debug trait, but for production use, it's safer to stick with the standard exposition. If you really need raw counts, consider using a separate collection like Vec<f64> and compute percentiles manually with a library like approx::percentile instead of relying on the histogram's built-in methods.
